stories.title: 22-Year-Old Black Woman Who Holds Multiple Guinness World Records Graduates From College
stories.summary: Wildine Aumoithe, a 22-year-old with a rare form of dwarfism, defied doctors' expectations and graduated from Liberty University. She also holds two Guinness World Records and became the first record holder to walk the runway at New York Fashion Week, championing adaptive fashion.
stories.extended_summary: In the documentary 'Born Different,' the world first saw Wildine Aumoithe, a young woman doctors said wouldn't survive 24 hours after birth. At four feet tall and reliant on a wheelchair, she has since earned multiple Guinness World Records, graduated college with a graphic design degree, and made history as the first record holder to model at New York Fashion Week. Her journey from a grim prognosis to a public advocate for disability representation shows what happens when human will outpaces medical expectations.

stories.title: Chiwaula Youth Turn from Charcoal Burning to Briquette Making for Income and Forest Protection
stories.summary: In Malawi's Chiwaula village, youth have swapped destructive charcoal burning for eco-friendly briquette making. The switch, supported by the YAGA project, turned a near-bankrupt club into a profitable enterprise while protecting the forest.
stories.extended_summary: The smell of burning charcoal once defined Chiwaula village as young people cut trees to survive. Then 18 members of the Chiwaula Youth Club learned to turn maize husks and cassava peels into briquettes. Within months, they earned over 600,000 kwacha — a life-changing sum that let them support families without destroying their environment. 'We thought there was no future here,' says chairperson Chisomo Nkhoma Umande. 'Now we are making money, protecting trees, and helping our families.'

stories.title: Flying Solo: The Rise of China’s One-Person Companies
stories.summary: Laid-off from his design job, Ying Junjiu used AI coding tools to build a productivity app entirely by himself. He's part of a rising wave of 'one-person companies' in China, where young founders are using AI to launch software without teams.
stories.extended_summary: Ying Junjiu, a UI designer who couldn't code, lost his job in 2024. Instead of scrambling for a new position, he turned to AI tools like Cursor and Codex to build Momoyu Timer, a playful app that tracks screen time. Within two years, he launched it on the App Store — a feat that once required a full engineering team. Across China, laid-off and ambitious young people are following suit, using artificial intelligence to start 'one-person companies' that challenge the need for traditional employment.
stories.source_url: https://www.sixthtone.com/news/1018567/Flying Solo: The Rise of China’s One-Person Companies

stories.title: For More Successful Farming, Follow The Forest
stories.summary: When twin brothers Alessandro and Nicola Giuggioli saw their crops wilting under 100-degree heat, they noticed the forest thrived. Inspired, they adopted a forest-inspired farming approach that restored soil health and boosted yield on their Italian farm.
stories.extended_summary: On a blistering summer day in Umbria, Alessandro Giuggioli stepped from his scorched tomato field into the adjacent woodland and felt cool, damp soil beneath his feet. While his crops boiled, the forest was flourishing. That contrast sparked a radical shift: the brothers began farming 'like nature would' — integrating trees, regenerating soil, and mimicking forest ecosystems. Their first harvests were small, but the land slowly healed, and diversity returned. Today, Quintosapore farm is a living experiment in climate-resilient agriculture, proving that sometimes the best teacher is the forest itself.

stories.title: Lucknow’s Street Vendors Work Long Hours in Extreme Heat. This 22-YO Is Bringing Them Shade
stories.summary: As heatwaves bake Lucknow, 22-year-old Khushi Pandey's Project Chhanv is distributing umbrellas to street vendors who work under the blazing sun. The small shade shields are giving workers dignity and the ability to earn without suffering.
stories.extended_summary: Street vendors in Lucknow spend up to 12 hours a day under a sun that feels like a furnace. For them, stepping away means lost income. Then Khushi Pandey, just 22, launched Project Chhanv through her NGO, handing out large umbrellas to 264 vendors so far. The umbrellas don't just block UV rays — they offer a patch of dignity in a city that often overlooks its roadside workers. 'Now I can work without my head spinning from the heat,' one vendor said.

stories.title: Mr. ChimeTime Went From Verizon Employee To Popular Food Influencer
stories.summary: After a pay cut and a COVID bout, Rashad Morton left Verizon to become Mr. ChimeTime, a food influencer who calls out bad restaurants. He turned a $1,000 monthly Facebook ad investment into a full-time business with a support team.
stories.extended_summary: Rashad Morton was making $140,000 at Verizon until a restructure slashed his income by nearly two-thirds. When COVID hit, he traded his corporate badge for a phone camera and started reviewing food — honestly, even when it stung. He poured $1,000 a month into Facebook ads to boost struggling local restaurants during lockdowns. Today, 'Mr. ChimeTime' has a full team and a platform built on brutal honesty. 'A lot of these restaurants do suck,' he says. 'And people appreciate hearing that.'

stories.title: Shorewood grad earns two college degrees in three years
stories.summary: Hayley Berkman, 20, is graduating from UW Seattle with two degrees and a medical assistant certification — all earned in three years. She racked up 324 college credits using Running Start, AP classes, and sheer determination.
stories.extended_summary: Hayley Berkman doesn't own a time-turner, but she built something close. By taking Running Start courses in high school, she entered the University of Washington with 90 credits. Over the next three years, she simultaneously earned a theater degree, a biology degree, and a medical assistant certificate — totaling 324 credits. 'I just kept telling myself that if I could make it through the week, eventually the weeks would turn into a degree,' she says. With her BA and BS in hand, she's now applying to medical school.

stories.title: Siblings' passion leads to dancing with the seniors
stories.summary: Sisters Sameea and Esha Patel turned their love for Kathak dance into The Movement Project, leading dance sessions for seniors at assisted living facilities. What started at one home now serves seven, with 50 teen volunteers.
stories.extended_summary: At The Birches in Clarendon Hills, residents who once sat through bingo now sway to Kathak beats. Sisters Sameea and Esha Patel, both competitive dancers, started leading seated and standing dance programs in November 2024. The sessions were so popular that they recruited friends and expanded to seven facilities. 'We tied what we love with connecting to older generations,' Esha says. Today, 50 teen volunteers from eight high schools help seniors move, smile, and feel younger — one dance step at a time.

stories.title: The 22-YO from Bihar Who Carried a Forgotten Bhojpuri Folk Song to One of India's Biggest Music Platforms
stories.summary: Utpal Udit, a 22-year-old from Bihar, rescued a 200-year-old Bhojpuri folk song from obscurity and brought it to Coke Studio Bharat. The track tells the story of a woman whose husband was shipped off to war by the British.
stories.extended_summary: In the narrow lanes of old Banaras, a woman watches her husband being marched away by the British Army, bound for a war in Rangoon. That forgotten grief became the heart of 'Kachaudi Gali,' a Bhojpuri folk song revived by Utpal Udit, a 22-year-old from Saharsa. He grew up hearing the region's raw, poetic folk music, but saw it reduced to stereotypes online. So he dug deep, found the song — set during the First Anglo-Burmese War — and brought it to Coke Studio Bharat Season 4. The track has become a viral anthem, restoring pride in a culture often flattened into clichés.

stories.title: UP Youth Builds Solar-Powered 7-Seater Using Scrap for Just Rs 10,000
stories.summary: 22-year-old Asad Abdullah built a seven-seater solar-powered car from scrap metal for just Rs 10,000. It runs over 200 kilometers on sunlight alone, proving low-cost sustainable transport is possible.
stories.extended_summary: In a small workshop in Azamgarh, Uttar Pradesh, a pile of discarded metal became a seven-seater vehicle — powered entirely by the sun. Asad Abdullah, 22, spent months welding, testing, and failing until his solar-powered creation could travel over 200 kilometers without a drop of fuel. Made for just Rs 10,000, it looks nothing like a showroom model, but it moves. And it keeps moving as long as there's daylight. 'If there is sunlight, it doesn't need fuel,' Asad says simply, proving that radical innovation doesn't require a big budget — just big thinking.

stories.title: Window Washer Turns Squeegee into Secret Weapon for Struggling KC Restaurants
stories.summary: Davis Roethler, co-owner of Window Wolf, cleans windows for KC restaurants for free—but he's really there to tell their stories. Using social media and Meta glasses, he highlights the people behind the food, helping struggling businesses gain exposure and customers.
stories.extended_summary: Davis Roethler doesn't just wash windows—he turns them into a lifeline for Kansas City restaurants. Armed with a squeegee and Meta glasses, he cleans for free while filming the real stories of owners and cooks. His videos, longer than typical food content, pull back the curtain on the people behind the plates: pit masters, families, and immigrants pouring passion into every meal. With nearly 9,000 followers on Instagram, his storytelling has given struggling eateries like Dunn Deal BBQ and Tasty African Food KC a surge in customers. It's a brilliant mix of generosity and strategic vision that's reshaping how small restaurants get noticed.

stories.headline_split: Window Washer Turns Squeegee into
fake_endings: a new weapon in the war on graffiti
a clever tool to help bees pollinate
stories.fake_headline: Former UPS Driver Transforms Delivery Truck into Mobile Library for Rural Communities

stories.title: ‘Cure for Paranoia’ frontman hopes NPR Tiny Desk win helps Texas do better for its artists
stories.summary: Cameron McCloud, frontman of Dallas hip-hop collective Cure for Paranoia, won NPR's Tiny Desk Contest after years of banking on the win. But he says he made his own success first—and that's what made the difference, teaching him to trust his voice and his daily rap posts.
stories.extended_summary: Cameron McCloud's Tiny Desk Contest win is the culmination of a decade-long dream—but it's his daily discipline that made it real. The Cure for Paranoia frontman spent 2025 posting a rap verse every day on social media, growing his following from 30,000 to 450,000 and building a deep trust in his own voice. By the time NPR called, he was already a success on his own terms. Now he's using the win to advocate for better support for Texas artists, hoping the state recognizes the talent it has. His story is a masterclass in betting on yourself before the big break arrives.
stories.headline_split: ‘Cure for Paranoia’ frontman hopes NPR Tiny Desk win helps Texas
stories.fake_headline: Retired Nurse Opens Free Clinic in Rural Montana Using Telemedicine and Volunteer Doctors

stories.title: ‘This is Serious Business’: The No-Cash Seed Loans Transforming the Lives of Malawi’s Young Farmers
stories.summary: In Malawi, a cooperative scheme is swapping cash loans for soya bean seeds, helping young farmers like Kondwani Dayimoni escape poverty. Instead of cash, participants receive high-quality seeds, bypassing traditional banks and middlemen to build sustainable businesses.
stories.extended_summary: Kondwani Dayimoni was about to drop out of school when he joined a program that gave him soya bean seeds instead of cash. Through the PUSHA loan initiative, young farmers in Ntchisi district access capital that can't be swallowed by immediate needs. Dayimoni's one-and-a-half-acre plot of soya beans now funds his tuition at teachers college. This clever twist on lending is transforming agriculture from a dead-end survival strategy into a launchpad for entrepreneurship, proving sometimes the best loan doesn't come with dollar signs. It's a model that absorbs risk and keeps young people invested in their own futures.
stories.headline_split: ‘This is Serious Business’: The No-Cash Seed Loans Transforming
stories.fake_headline: Elementary School Students Launch Community Garden to Feed Families in Food Desert

stories.title: A ‘symphony’ of wildlife suggests carbon financing is working in Sierra Leone
stories.summary: A new study finds that carbon financing in Sierra Leone's Gola Rainforest is not just protecting trees—it's also boosting animal biodiversity. The park's rich soundscape of birds, primates, and drumming chimpanzees suggests the economic measures are working for wildlife too.
stories.extended_summary: H.S. Sathya Chandra Sagar traveled to Gola Rainforest National Park expecting to study carbon credits—but what he heard stopped him. The forest was alive with the calls of birds, hoots of primates, and the distant drumming of chimpanzees. His research, published in Conservation Science and Practice, shows that the UN's REDD+ carbon financing project is protecting more than just carbon: it's preserving a symphony of animal life. The study is one of the first to confirm that good climate policy can also be good for biodiversity.
stories.headline_split: A ‘symphony’ of wildlife suggests carbon financing is working
stories.fake_headline: Teenager Creates App That Connects Food Banks with Surplus Produce from Local Farms

stories.title: Berkeley land trust aims to keep artists housed in the Bay Area
stories.summary: A new Berkeley nonprofit helped 27-year-old artist Jaelynn Walls buy their first home in Oakland with $168,000 in downpayment assistance. Artist Space Trust aims to keep creatives housed in the Bay Area—one artist at a time.
stories.extended_summary: Jaelynn Walls thought homeownership was years away—they were crammed in a Tenderloin studio, spending most of their income on rent. Then they saw an Instagram post from Artist Space Trust, a new Berkeley nonprofit that helps artists secure affordable housing. Within three months, Walls and their wife got the keys to a three-bedroom in East Oakland. The trust helped them land $168,000 from CalHOME, a state program. Now Walls has space to write their next young-adult novel, quilt, and host out-of-town artist friends. Their mortgage takes a fraction of their income. 'This is the greatest thing that has ever happened to me,' they say. Artist Space Trust is proving that keeping artists in the Bay Area isn't a dream—it's a blueprint.
stories.headline_split: Berkeley land trust aims to keep artists housed
stories.fake_headline: New York Program Provides Free Rent for Teachers in High-Cost Neighborhoods

stories.title: It is time to Pawty
stories.summary: Instead of birthday presents, 10-year-old LiliAna LeShay is asking for donations to her local animal shelter. The community is stepping up to fill her car with food and supplies—proving that the best gifts are the ones you give.
stories.extended_summary: LiliAna LeShay doesn't want toys for her birthday—she wants to fill her trunk with dog food. The Fayette County shelter volunteer is rallying her town to donate supplies, with drop-off spots at pizza joints and the shelter itself. 'It's a community effort,' she says, and the party livestream on June 11 will show just how much impact one kid can make. Sometimes the best birthday gift is a wagging tail.
stories.headline_split: It is time to
stories.fake_headline: Local Rescue Dog Becomes First Canine to Earn a PhD in Fetching

stories.title: Meet a Health Hero: John Matthews of Ride Hard, Breathe Easy
stories.summary: After his mom's lung cancer diagnosis, John Matthews biked across America and turned his journey into Ride Hard, Breathe Easy. The nonprofit has raised $1.2 million for patients, families, and research—and he's just getting started.
stories.extended_summary: John Matthews pedaled coast to coast for his mother, and he never stopped. His nonprofit, Ride Hard, Breathe Easy, has now raised $1.2 million to fight lung cancer, a disease that kills more people than any other cancer but gets far less attention. Matthews fights the stigma too: anyone with lungs can get lung cancer. From funding research to supporting families, he's turning grief into action—and his bike is still rolling.
stories.headline_split: Meet a Health Hero: John Matthews of Ride Hard,
stories.fake_headline: Veteran Bike Mechanic Teaches Kids to Build Their Own Bicycles

stories.title: Orcutt students graduate with high school diplomas and Hancock associate degrees in a new college readiness program
stories.summary: 26 students from Orcutt Academy High School graduated with not just diplomas but two associate degrees from Allan Hancock College. Through the Early College Cadre program, they started college classes as freshmen and built a path to higher education together.
stories.extended_summary: Jackson Payne was a freshman when he took his first college class at 7 a.m. in his high school. Four years later, he and 25 classmates earned two associate degrees from Allan Hancock College before even getting their high school diplomas. The Early College Cadre program had professors travel to the high school for zero period classes and summer sessions, creating a tight-knit cohort that grew together. It's a model that proves high school students can handle college-level work—and that the payoff is enormous.
stories.headline_split: Orcutt students graduate with high school diplomas and
stories.fake_headline: Grandmother of 12 Earns GED, Then Goes on to Get Master's Degree in Social Work

stories.title: Pianist will perform outdoors at BBR
stories.summary: Classical pianist Hunter Noack is bringing his 'In A Landscape' concert series to Black Butte Ranch this June. Audiences listen through wireless headphones while wandering meadows and mountains, experiencing music with nature as the backdrop—a perfect blend of his two passions.
stories.extended_summary: Hunter Noack grew up hunting and fishing in Central Oregon, always longing to combine his love of the outdoors with classical music. Now he tours with a 1912 Steinway Model D, performing in meadows and old-growth forests where audiences roam freely with wireless headphones. His 'In A Landscape' series returns to Black Butte Ranch June 15-17, offering a chance to hear Debussy against a backdrop of mountains and grass. It's a concert experience that redefines where music belongs—and it's been a hit for over a decade.
stories.headline_split: Pianist will perform outdoors
stories.fake_headline: Flutist Gives Impromptu Recital at Bus Station, Goes Viral

stories.title: Sisters give famous masterpieces a makeover with dog kibble in new art exhibit
stories.summary: MIDTOWN, Manhattan (WABC) -- Move over Monet, New York's latest art exhibit is going to the dogs. At the American Kennel Club Museum of the Dog, some of the world's most famous masterpieces are getting a furry, yet foodie-inspired makeover. Think works of art recreated entirely out of dog kibble. You'll find the art inside the museum located on 40th Street just off Park. Sisters, Lauren and Samant
stories.extended_summary: MIDTOWN, Manhattan (WABC) -- Move over Monet, New York's latest art exhibit is going to the dogs. At the American Kennel Club Museum of the Dog, some of the world's most famous masterpieces are getting a furry, yet foodie-inspired makeover. Think works of art recreated entirely out of dog kibble. You'll find the art inside the museum located on 40th Street just off Park. Sisters, Lauren and Samantha Treadwell, from Old Bridge, New Jersey, created the pieces, partnering with Royal Canin. The ladies chose the classic images from a long list and used stucco and polyurethane to keep things in place and preserved. "We're painters, this was kind of like the learning curve of like how mosaic artists use glass and break and move it into different pieces and make different shapes," Samantha Treadwe

stories.title: Summer Street students strive to end hunger
stories.summary: For five years, fourth-grader Alex Hayes and his classmates have collected food for The Open Door food pantry in Gloucester. This year, the whole grade participated in their biggest drive yet, turning a kindergarten lesson in philanthropy into a lasting tradition.
stories.extended_summary: Alex Hayes was in kindergarten when he and his mom first learned about The Open Door food pantry. Five years later, he and his classmates have made the food drive a schoolwide tradition—and this year's collection was the biggest ever. The fourth-grade class, including Alex who had heart surgery two years ago, loaded bags under the blazing sun with smiles. Their teacher says it's become a ritual that teaches compassion and community, proving that even young kids can make a real dent in hunger.
stories.headline_split: Summer Street students strive to
stories.fake_headline: Oakwood Students Create Buddy System for New Immigrant Families

stories.title: Welding Just For Men, Right? Not Exactly. Go Mikala!
stories.summary: Mikala Sposito, 21, from Dexter, Michigan, will be the first woman ever to represent the United States in welding at the WorldSkills Competition in China. After winning the USA Weld Trials, she's heading to the 'Olympics of skilled trades.'
stories.extended_summary: Welding is often seen as a man's world—but Mikala Sposito is about to change that on a global stage. The 21-year-old from Dexter, Michigan, won the USA Weld Trials and will represent the United States at the WorldSkills Competition in China, the first woman ever to do so. A student at Washtenaw Community College, she worked tirelessly to hone her skills, proving that sparks and steel have no gender. Her achievement shines a light on the growing number of women in trades and the power of perseverance.
stories.headline_split: Welding Just For Men, Right? Not Exactly.
stories.fake_headline: Auto Repair Isn't Just for Men? Meet Jenny, the Engine Whisperer

stories.title: BC alum Welles Crowther to receive Presidential Medal of Freedom posthumously
stories.summary: Welles Crowther, known as 'The Man in the Red Bandana' for guiding people to safety on 9/11, will receive the Presidential Medal of Freedom. The Boston College graduate and former lacrosse player saved up to 18 lives before the South Tower collapsed.
stories.extended_summary: Welles Crowther was just 24 when he made the ultimate choice on September 11, 2001. Wearing his signature red bandana, he led people down smoke-filled stairs in the South Tower, saving as many as 18 lives before the building fell. For over two decades, his story has inspired millions. Now, President Trump announced he will posthumously receive the Presidential Medal of Freedom, the nation's highest civilian honor. His mother, Alison, accepted the news with gratitude—a testament to a hero who never sought recognition.

stories.headline_split: BC alum Welles Crowther to receive
stories.fake_headline: Stanford Alumnus Awarded Nobel Prize in Physics for Breakthrough in Quantum Computing

stories.title: Chicago comedians could get help buying health insurance from new fundraising alliance
stories.summary: A new pilot program will help 10 Chicago comedians pay their health insurance premiums. Co-founder Elizabeth Grossman started the Health Alliance for Chicago Comedians after hearing about a similar effort for musicians, addressing the financial strain that comes with gig work.
stories.extended_summary: Stand-up comic Deanna Ortiz juggles multiple gigs just to cover rent and her $80 monthly health insurance premium. She's one of the many Chicago comedians struggling with healthcare costs — a problem Elizabeth Grossman noticed while taking a comedy class. Grossman co-founded the Health Alliance for Chicago Comedians, a pilot program that will cover insurance premiums for 10 local comics through the next ACA open enrollment. The initiative mirrors a model used for musicians in Texas and aims to show that comedy deserves a place in arts advocacy. For Ortiz, it could mean less anxiety about affording her asthma inhaler — and more focus on making people laugh.

stories.title: Corvallis’ Kimberlee Barker Selected to Represent USA at World Equestrian Championship
stories.summary: Kimberlee Barker is heading to Spain this fall to represent the USA at the World Equestrian Championship. She's one of four riders on the first-ever U.S. Working Equitation team, transitioning from dressage in 2019. Barker qualified with her horse Nairobi and aims to set a strong first impression for American teams in the sport.
stories.extended_summary: Imagine being the first ever to represent your country in a sport — that's Kimberlee Barker's reality. The Corvallis rider and coach earned a spot on the inaugural U.S. Working Equitation team for the world championship in Spain. She qualified with Nairobi, an 8-year-old Lusitano gelding she imported from Portugal in 2024. Barker, who switched from dressage to this discipline in 2019, knows she's not just competing — she's setting the stage for future American teams. 'There will never be another first American team,' she said.
stories.headline_split: Corvallis’ Kimberlee Barker Selected to Represent

stories.title: From the Kitchen to Campus Leadership: Dr. Andy Oguntola Shares His Recipe for Success
stories.summary: Dr. Andy Oguntola went from arriving at college with a trash bag of belongings to becoming assistant vice president of admissions at Florida Polytech. Now he's published a leadership book that hit #1 on the Christian Leadership bestseller list. His 'THANKFUL Method' shares the ingredients for becoming a trusted leader.
stories.extended_summary: Andy Oguntola showed up at Webber University with a suitcase and a black trash bag — no bed sheet, no plan beyond a football scholarship. That scholarship didn't pan out, but a job offer in admissions a week before graduation changed everything. Today he's Dr. Andy Oguntola, Assistant Vice President of Admissions at Florida Polytech, and his new book 'The Right Ingredients' just hit #1 on the Christian Leadership bestseller list. He remembers sitting in his mother's kitchen, learning that successful recipes — like leadership — need the right ingredients. Now he's teaching that lesson to the world.

stories.title: Project Rebound Scholar Accepted to Columbia Master’s Program
stories.summary: DeShawn SirajanMuneer-Hunter spent 19 years incarcerated, but he just earned his bachelor's degree from Cal State Fullerton and is headed to Columbia University for a master's in social work. With support from the Project Rebound program, he's beating the odds and aiming for a law degree and doctorate next.
stories.extended_summary: DeShawn SirajanMuneer-Hunter made a sacred promise to his late mother: 'I was more than a statistic.' After 19 years behind bars, he walked across the graduation stage at Cal State Fullerton with a bachelor's in human services. And he's not stopping — this fall he starts a master's in social work at Columbia University, with plans to pursue both a law degree and a PhD from Columbia Law School. He credits the Project Rebound program for housing and community support that made his journey possible. 'It showed me I am not alone.'

stories.title: The only full-time spelling bee coach charges $180 an hour. Champs say he's worth it
stories.summary: Scott Remer is the only full-time professional spelling bee coach in the U.S., charging $180 an hour. He's coached five national champions, including Faizan Zaki and Dev Shah. With 34 students this year, Remer has turned elite spelling into a career — and his students keep winning.
stories.extended_summary: When Faizan Zaki and Dev Shah won back-to-back Scripps National Spelling Bees, the same guy was standing next to them in an aloha shirt: Scott Remer. He's the country's only full-time, professional spelling coach — charging $180 an hour and building a business around it. Remer, 32, has coached five national champions and this year has 34 spellers in the competition. Most coaches are former spellers still in college, but Remer has turned coaching into a full-blown career. 'He's probably one of the most influential figures in spelling over the past 10 years,' said Shah.

stories.title: There's a new T. rex from the dinosaur age — and it ruled the seas with a skull-crushing bite
stories.summary: Scientists have named a new species of mosasaur — a marine reptile — Tylosaurus rex. It measured up to 43 feet long and had serrated teeth and powerful jaws. Discovered in Texas decades ago, the fossils reveal a top predator that ruled the seas 80 million years ago.
stories.extended_summary: Meet Tylosaurus rex — a 43-foot-long marine predator with finely serrated teeth and a skull-crushing bite. Scientists just named this new mosasaur species from fossils discovered decades ago in northern Texas. Researchers at the American Museum of Natural History noticed a specimen labeled as a known species didn't quite match, leading to the discovery. 'Everything is bigger in Texas and that includes the mosasaurs,' said study lead Amelia Zietlow. At 80 million years old, this ancient sea monster still has new secrets to reveal.

stories.title: This Chilean startup and Copec join forces to reduce energy consumption in air conditioning by up to 40%
stories.summary: Chilean startup Flair has partnered with Copec to distribute its AI-powered HVAC optimization tech across Chile. The system cuts air conditioning energy consumption by up to 40% without replacing equipment. Clients like Codelco and Mall Plaza already validated the technology.
stories.extended_summary: A Chilean startup called Flair just got a massive boost: a partnership with energy giant Copec to roll out its AI-driven air conditioning optimization across the country. Founded in 2021, Flair integrates sensors and AI into existing HVAC systems to cut energy use by up to 40% — with no equipment replacement. Early clients included Codelco, Mall Plaza, and the University of Chile. 'The savings are seen in the next electricity bill,' said co-founder Pablo Farah. The deal brings smart efficiency to everything from convenience stores to supermarkets and clinics.
